perturbation.original_text: All Products manufactured, packaged and delivered by Heritage or Jasper under the terms of this Agreement shall conform to the specifications supplied to Heritage by Premier as listed on Schedule A and/or Schedule A-1, which Schedules may from time to time be modified by Premier in writing (the "Specifications"), shall conform to Post Holding's Quality Expectations Manual attached hereto as Schedule E, and shall conform in all material respects to samples previously supplied to Premier by Heritage.
perturbation.changed_text: All Products manufactured, packaged and delivered by Heritage or Jasper under the terms of this Agreement shall conform to the specifications supplied to Heritage by Premier as listed on Schedule A and/or Schedule A-1, which Schedules may from time to time be modified by Premier in writing (the "Specifications"), and shall conform in all material respects to samples previously supplied to Premier by Heritage.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that the products conform to Post Holding's Quality Expectations Manual attached as Schedule E, the contract creates a loophole that could allow for the manufacturing of substandard products. This contradicts consumer protection laws that mandate safe and merchantable goods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Rights Law - implied warranty of merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 2314 implies a warranty that goods shall be merchantable, meaning they must be fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. Removing the adherence to the Quality Expectations Manual weakens the assurance of product quality and could result in a breach of this implied warranty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for products to conform to Post Holding's Quality Expectations Manual, potentially leading to the manufacturing of substandard products. This contradicts California Commercial Code Section 2314, which implies a warranty that goods shall be merchantable and fit for their ordinary purposes. Specifically, subsection (2)(c) states that goods must be "fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used." By removing the quality expectations manual, the contract weakens the assurance of product quality, potentially resulting in a breach of the implied warranty of merchantability under COM 2314.

perturbation.original_text: Heritage shall purchase all ingredients and packaging materials identified in the relevant Schedule C or Schedule C-l to be used in connection with the manufacturer of the Products. Heritage shall invoice Premier through the [***] billing as identified on the relevant Schedule C or Schedule C-l. Heritage shall not, however, purchase ingredients or packaging materials in excess of those required [***].
perturbation.changed_text: Heritage shall purchase ingredients and packaging materials to be used in connection with the manufacturer of the Products. Heritage shall invoice Premier through the [***] billing. Heritage shall not, however, purchase ingredients or packaging materials in excess of those required [***].
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to 'relevant Schedule C or Schedule C-l', the contract becomes vague about which ingredients and packaging materials are approved. This omission introduces a risk of using non-compliant or substandard materials, potentially violating consumer safety regulations enforced by the FDA if the products are food or beverages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S.C. Â§ 342 defines adulterated food. By omitting the requirement to use specific ingredients and packaging as defined in the schedules, the risk of using unsafe or non-approved materials increases, potentially leading to the production of adulterated food, which is a violation of federal law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that Heritage purchases ingredients and packaging materials identified in Schedule C or C-l, ensuring compliance with predefined standards. The changed contract omits this reference, increasing the risk of using non-compliant materials. The Law Explanation states that this omission could lead to the production of adulterated food, violating federal law (21 U.S.C. Â§ 342, as referenced in the Law Explanation). While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly state this law, it contains text referring to potential errors in documents. Because of the omission, the contract now contradicts guidelines ensuring product safety by potentially allowing the use of unapproved materials, which are otherwise outlined in the original text.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term, Premier shall have the right (but not the obligation) to order from Heritage quantities of Products in in excess of [***] and provided Heritage has the capacity and the ability to produce such additional quantities of Products, Heritage agrees to produce such additional quantities per the pricing and terms on Schedule C.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term, Premier shall have the right (but not the obligation) to order from Heritage quantities of Products in in excess of [***] and provided Heritage has the capacity and the ability to produce such additional quantities of Products, Heritage agrees to produce such additional quantities.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'per the pricing and terms on Schedule C' creates ambiguity regarding the pricing for additional quantities. This omission could violate California's consumer protection laws related to clear and transparent pricing, especially if the lack of clarity leads to unfair pricing practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code Â§ 1770 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices. By removing the reference to agreed-upon pricing (Schedule C) for additional product quantities, the contract opens the door for arbitrary and potentially unfair pricing, which could be construed as a violation of this Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1770 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person does not have
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'per the pricing and terms on Schedule C' directly contradicts California Civil Code Â§ 1770(a), which prohibits unfair methods of competition and deceptive practices. By removing the pricing agreement reference, the changed text creates ambiguity, opening the door to potentially unfair or deceptive pricing, as prices are no longer tied to a pre-agreed schedule. The scraped snippet specifically mentions 'unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices' as unlawful, which directly aligns with the potential consequence of the omission.
